Its most striking feature is the **arched dorsal shield**, a clear, glassy dome that rises from its back like a polished blister of amber. This shell is not true glass but a layered mineralized biological composite: an outer surface rich in silica crystals, beneath which lies a flexible lattice of collagen-like fibers and living tissue. Sunlight passes through the dome and into a spread of dark, branching membranes beneath it. These membranes are photosynthetic symbiont organs—part plantlike, part microbial colony—living in partnership with the animal. Because of this, the crawler is not purely an animal in the Earth sense; it is a **mobile symbiotic grazer**, getting energy both by eating and by harvesting light. The shell therefore acts as both armor and greenhouse.

The underside is entirely different. Instead of a hard belly, the glassback has a dense mat of **muscular feeding cilia and gripping pads**, arranged in six radial limb-clusters. Each limb is short and thick at the base, dividing at the end into many fingerlike tendrils. These tendrils can probe cracks in rocks, rake up microbial crusts, and shovel powdered organic material into a ventral feeding groove. Its movement resembles a slow coordinated ripple, as each limb-cluster anchors, contracts, and releases. On loose dunes, the tendrils spread wide like snowshoes; on stone, they grip with tiny hooklike keratinous nodules.

At the front is a **sensory crown** rather than a head in the familiar vertebrate sense. A ring of twelve flexible stalks surrounds a beaklike mouth. Each stalk bears patches of different receptor cells: some detect vibration through the ground, others sense changes in air chemistry, polarized light, humidity, or heat. The animal has no obvious eyes, but two larger stalks contain spherical sacs filled with layered photoreceptive gels, allowing it to form crude images and detect motion. These “eye sacs” are poor at detail but excellent at spotting shadows and navigating by the angle of the sun and stars. The rest of the crown constantly sways and samples its environment, giving the impression that the front of the creature is always “thinking” with its skin.

This is adapted to scrape lichenlike growths, crack brittle nodules full of dormant spores, and crush the shelled burrowers that emerge after rare rains. Behind the beak lies a **triple-chambered digestive tract**. The first chamber mechanically grinds food with silicate grit. The second is acidic and breaks down tougher tissues. The third is a fermenting sac packed with symbiotic microorganisms that extract nutrients from otherwise indigestible polysaccharides and mineral-rich biofilms. In this way the glassback crawler is an omnivorous detritivore and opportunistic predator, though slow enough that most active prey is taken only if trapped or uncovered.

Internally, its body is supported not by a rigid skeleton but by a **hydrostatic-muscular framework** reinforced with flexible struts of mineralized cartilage. This gives it a body that is firm but deformable: it can flatten itself during storms, wedge into crevices, or slightly inflate parts of its body to right itself if overturned. Its tissues are threaded with channels carrying a copper-based oxygen transport fluid, giving exposed flesh a blue-green cast.
